critique.summary: The article announces the release of Kitten TTS v0.8, an open-source, lightweight text-to-speech library with models ranging from 15M to 80M parameters and sizes between 25 MB and 80 MB. It highlights the library's CPU-optimized performance, multiple built-in voices, and features such as adjustable speech speed and text preprocessing.
mainPoints: Kitten TTS v0.8 offers three model sizes: 15M (nano), 40M (micro), and 80M (mini) parameters.
Models range in size from 25 MB (int8 quantized nano model) to 80 MB, suitable for edge deployment without requiring a GPU.
The library supports 8 built-in voices and outputs high-quality 24 kHz audio.
It is CPU-optimized using ONNX for efficient inference.
The project is open-source under the Apache 2.0 license and provides commercial support options.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Kitten TTS are consistent with publicly available information on the official GitHub repository and related documentation. The model sizes, parameter counts, and features such as CPU optimization and multiple voices are accurately described. The project is indeed open-source and uses ONNX for inference. The mention of commercial support and the license type also align with the repository details. There is no indication of exaggerated or false claims.
considerations: User experience and audio quality comparisons with other TTS models are not discussed.
Potential limitations or issues, such as those reported with the int8 quantized nano model, are briefly mentioned but not elaborated.
The article does not provide independent benchmarks or third-party reviews of the model's performance.
Compatibility with various operating systems and integration complexity for developers is only briefly mentioned.
The roadmap and future development plans could impact the stability and features of the library.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the new Kitten TTS models, highlighting their small size and usability for edge devices. Users discuss performance, voice quality, dependency issues, and potential applications, with some requesting more details on training data and features.

comments.author: baibai008989
comments.text:
the dependency chain issue is a real barrier for edge deployment. i've been running tts models on a raspberry pi for a home automation project and anything that pulls torch + cuda makes the whole thing a non-starter. 25MB is genuinely exciting for that use case.

curious about the latency characteristics though. 1.5x realtime on a 9700 is fine for batch processing but for interactive use you need first-chunk latency under 200ms or the conversation feels broken. does anyone know if it supports streaming output or is it full-utterance only?

the phoneme-based approach should help with pronunciation consistency too. the models i've tried that work on raw text tend to mispronounce technical terms unpredictably — same word pronounced differently across runs.
comments.analysis: This comment provides practical insight from experience, highlighting a real challenge in deploying TTS models on low-power devices due to heavy dependencies.

comments.author: bobokaytop
comments.text: The size/quality tradeoff here is interesting. 25MB for a TTS model that's usable is a real achievement, but the practical bottleneck for most edge deployments isn't model size -- it's the inference latency on low-power hardware and the audio streaming architecture around it. Curious how this performs on something like a Raspberry Pi 4 for real-time synthesis. The voice quality tradeoff at that size usually shows up most in prosody and sentence-final intonation rather than phoneme accuracy.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately identifies the main challenge in edge TTS deployment as latency rather than model size, reflecting an understanding of practical constraints.

comments.author: kevin42
comments.text:
What I love about OpenClaw is that I was able to send it a message on Discord with just this github URL and it started sending me voice messages using it within a few minutes. It also gave me a bunch of different benchmarks and sample audio.

I'm impressed with the quality given the size. I don't love the voices, but it's not bad. Running on an intel 9700 CPU, it's about 1.5x realtime using the 80M model. It wasn't any faster running on a 3080 GPU though.
comments.analysis: Provides useful benchmark data and a balanced opinion on voice quality, contributing valuable experiential feedback.

comments.author: dawdler-purge
comments.text:
I created a CLI wrapper for Kitten TTS: https://github.com/newptcai/purr

BTW, it seems that kitten (the Python package) has the following chain of dependencies: kittentts → misaki[en] → spacy-curated-transformers

So if you install it directly via uv, it will pull torch and NVIDIA CUDA packages (several GB), which are not needed to run kitten.
comments.analysis: Highlights an important technical detail about unnecessary heavy dependencies, which is relevant for users concerned about deployment size.

comments.author: __fst__
comments.text:
Was playing around a bit and for its size it's very impressive. Just has issues pronounciating numbers. I tried to let it generate "Startup finished in 135 ms."

I didn't expect it to pronounciate 'ms' correctly, but the number sounded just like noise. Eventually I got an acceptable result for the string "Startup finished in one hundred and thirty five seconds.
comments.analysis: Provides a specific and practical observation about pronunciation limitations, useful for understanding model weaknesses.

comments.author: altruios
comments.text:
One of the core features I look for is expressive control.

Either in the form of the api via pitch/speed/volume controls, for more deterministic controls.

Or in expressive tags such as [coughs], [urgently], or [laughs in melodic ascending and descending arpeggiated gibberish babbles].

the 25MB model is amazingly good for being 25MB. How does it handle expressive tags?
comments.analysis: Raises a relevant question about expressive control features, indicating an understanding of advanced TTS capabilities.

comments.author: jacquesm
comments.text: Good on device TTS is an amazing accessibility tool. Thank you for building this. Way too many of devices that use it rely on online services, this is much preferred.
comments.analysis: Emphasizes the importance of offline TTS for accessibility, reflecting a user-centered perspective.

comments.author: ks2048
comments.text:
There's a number of recent, good quality, small TTS models.

If the author doesn't describe some detail about the data, training, or a novel architecture, etc, I only assume they just took another one, do a little finetuning, and repackage as a new product.
comments.analysis: Expresses skepticism about novelty without detailed disclosures, a common concern in ML model releases.

comments.author: Remi_Etien
comments.text: 25MB is impressive. What's the tradeoff vs the 80M model — is it mainly voice quality or does it also affect pronunciation accuracy on less common words?
comments.analysis: Asks a pertinent question about tradeoffs between model sizes, showing a focus on practical performance differences.

comments.author: rsmtjohn
comments.text:
The <25MB figure is what stands out. Been wanting to add TTS to a few Next.js projects for offline/edge scenarios but model sizes have always made it impractical to ship.

At 25MB you can actually bundle it with the app. Going to test whether this works in a Vercel Edge Function context -- if latency is acceptable there it opens up a lot of use cases that currently require a round-trip to a hosted API.
comments.analysis: Highlights the practical significance of the small model size for edge computing and offline use cases.
